channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: As Delegations Gather in Qatar For Talks, U.S. Strikes Iran’s Gulf Coast
item.description: Iranian officials went to Doha on Monday for the negotiations, hours before the U.S. military announced the strikes it said were intended to protect troops. Israel’s leader said his country plans to intensify attacks against Hezbollah, Iran’s ally, in Lebanon.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Iceland, Rattled by Trump’s Greenland Threats, Weighs Joining the E.U.
item.description: Iceland has stood apart from the rest of Europe. But President Trump’s threats to Greenland have provoked a reconsideration.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg and Sergey Ponomarev
item.media:credit: Sergey Ponomarev for The New York Times

item.title: Inside India’s Manipur State, Where Violence and Division Are Routine
item.description: Three years after riots tore apart Manipur, the state remains in disarray. Barbed wire and armed checkpoints made it difficult for Times reporters to cross, even before the recent clashes.
item.dc:creator: Hari Kumar and Atul Loke
item.media:description: Security personnel at a buffer-zone checkpoint between Imphal and Churachandpur in Manipur, India, in March.

item.title: Pope Leo Warns of Risks From A.I. in 42,300-Word Encyclical
item.description: The document marks a powerful foray by the leader of the Roman Catholic Church into the debate about the misuse or overuse of artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Motoko Rich, Elisabetta Povoledo and Elizabeth Dias
item.media:description: Pope Leo XIV at the presentation of his first encyclical letter, “Magnifica Humanitas,” at the Vatican on Monday. In it, he outlined his desire to protect human dignity and agency in an age of artificial intelligence.

item.title: Cubans Cook With Charcoal and Wood Fires to Survive During Energy Crisis
item.description: The U.S. oil blockade has left millions without cooking gas. In Santiago de Cuba, the cradle of the Cuban revolution, apartment tower residents resort to charcoal and firewood.
item.dc:creator: Ed Augustin and Lisette Poole González
item.media:description: Outside one of five 18 story apartment towers in Santiago de Cuba this month.

item.title: A City in the Kill Zone
item.description: Little is left of Kostiantynivka, a city on the front lines. Rescuers risk their lives to ferry out the few who remain.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Hicks and Gaëlle Girbes

item.title: Cambodia’s Leader Pardons Top Opposition Politician Kem Sokha
item.description: The decision to release Kem Sokha is being seen as a likely attempt to recalibrate frayed ties with Western countries.
item.dc:creator: Sun Narin and Sui-Lee Wee
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Tang Chhin Sothy/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Kem Sokha, the leading opposition leader in Cambodia, arriving for the verdict in his trial in Phnom Penh in 2023.

item.title: How Wine, Truffles and Honey Could Help Europe Fight Wildfires
item.description: Countries are learning that the finer things in life sometimes have a serendipitous side benefit.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek
item.media:credit: Samuel Aranda for The New York Times

item.title: ‘A Dangerous Bluff’: Carney Likens Alberta Referendum to Brexit
item.description: The Canadian prime minister, who was the governor of the Bank of England when Britain voted to leave the European Union, said the Alberta referendum on separating from Canada could backfire.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:credit: Ethan Cairns/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada said of Brexit, “They’re still, 10 years later, trying to undo what people didn’t think they were voting for.”

item.title: In Ukraine, a Divisive 20th-Century Hero Comes Home
item.description: Andriy Melnyk, criticized as a Nazi collaborator and lionized as an anti-Soviet resistance leader, was given state honors for his reburial near Kyiv.
item.dc:creator: Andrew E. Kramer
item.media:credit: Andriy Zhyhaylo/Global Images Ukraine, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The coffins of Andriy Melnyk and his wife, Sofia Fedak-Melnyk, in Kyiv, Ukraine, on Friday.

item.title: In City at Center of Ebola Crisis, ‘the Virus Is Far Ahead of Us’
item.description: The deadly virus has spread alarmingly in Congo for months. Only now is the response taking shape.
item.dc:creator: Declan Walsh and Arlette Bashizi
item.media:description: Disinfecting the public market on Saturday in Bunia, in the Democratic Republic of Congo’s northeast, as part of the response to the Ebola virus’s resurgence.

item.title: Pope Leo Has Released an Encyclical About A.I. Why Is That Important?
item.description: Leo XIV issued his thoughts about artificial intelligence in the modern world using a centuries-old form of papal communication called an encyclical.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Povoledo
item.media:description: Pope Leo has made artificial intelligence a central theme of his first year as pontiff.

item.title: Israel’s President, Isaac Herzog, Denounces Israeli Violence
item.description: In a speech, Isaac Herzog, whose role is largely ceremonial, delivered a grave indictment of settler attacks in the West Bank and abuse of prisoners.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: President Isaac Herzog of Israel in Panama this month.

item.title: How a Curious FIFA Deal Gave Fox a Huge Bargain for World Cup Broadcast Rights
item.description: A decision to stave off litigation between Fox and FIFA turned into a bonanza worth hundreds of millions of dollars in discounted World Cup rights to the broadcaster.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.media:description: FIFA headquarters in Zurich in 2024.

item.title: Evolving Warfare Connects the Conflicts in Ukraine and Iran
item.description: Aspects such as drone technology and diplomacy show how the wars intersect on the battlefield and in global alignments, providing a model for future conflicts.
item.dc:creator: Lara Jakes
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Assessing the damaged buildings following Russian strikes in Ukraine’s capital, Kyiv, on Sunday.

item.title: U.S. Carries Out Renewed Strikes in Southern Iran
item.description: Military officials said that the strikes targeted missile sites near a major Iranian port that threatened U.S. ships.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Ships near Bandar Abbas, Iran, in May.

item.title: Netanyahu Says Israel Plans to Intensify Attacks on Hezbollah in Lebanon
item.description: After the prime minister made the announcement, the Israeli military said it had struck more than 70 Hezbollah sites in the past day.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Smoke rising in the city of Tyre, Lebanon, on Monday.

item.title: Are We Pandemic Ready?
item.description: Hantavirus and Ebola are reminders that outbreaks are inevitable, and that the world must work together to contain them and prevent the next pandemic.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times

item.title: Main Takeaways From Pope Leo’s Encyclical on A.I.
item.description: In his letter, the first American pontiff called for putting care for humans at the heart of technological change.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Graham and Elizabeth Dias
item.media:description: Pope Leo XIV speaks during the presentation of “Magnifica Humanitas,” his first encyclical, at the Vatican on Monday.

item.title: Trump’s Pressure Had Little Effect on Iran’s Terms for a Peace Deal
item.description: President Trump had sought to force Tehran to accept American demands for a peace deal with a mixture of threats and military operations.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: For weeks, the Trump administration pursued a diplomatic agreement rather than returning to war in Iran.

item.title: UK Heat Wave Could Break May Temperature Record
item.description: Forecasters warned that temperatures could climb to the highest level ever recorded in the month of May, reaching 35 degrees Celsius (95 degrees Fahrenheit) in some areas of England.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar
item.media:description: Beachgoers in Brighton, England, on Monday. The country is facing its first heat wave of the season.

item.title: A deal with the U.S. would be preliminary and focused on ending the war, an Iranian official says.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands and Sanam Mahoozi
item.media:description: Esmail Baghaei, Iran’s foreign ministry spokesman, gives a news conference in Tehran earlier this month.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman, Tyler Pager, Eric Schmitt, Ephrat Livni and Sanam Mahoozi

item.title: One of Madagascar’s Oldest Sacred Trees Is Slowly Dying
item.description: In a forest in Madagascar, the demise of a centuries-old baobab points to the fraying of a fragile ecosystem.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe

item.title: Rubio Rebukes Hezbollah Chief Over Call for Lebanese to ‘Take to the Streets’
item.description: The militant leader, in a speech, had criticized the Lebanese government for engaging in direct talks with Israel.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A building hit by an Israeli strike in Tyre, Lebanon, on Saturday.

item.title: The Oscars Start at Cannes
item.description: Glamorous and a touch old-fashioned, the French film festival increasingly defines what Hollywood celebrates each year.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Senate Republicans Cast Doubt on a Potential Peace Deal With Iran
item.description: Lawmakers, including some of President Trump’s closest allies, slammed the emerging agreement as effectively undermining the president’s own war goals.
item.dc:creator: Minho Kim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Senator Thom Tillis said it was “questionable” that Iran would end its blockade on the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Rubio Says Details on Iran Nuclear Program Still to Be Negotiated
item.description: The Secretary of State said the United States was prepared to begin those talks if Iran opens the Strait of Hormuz as part of an apparently emerging deal on the war.
item.dc:creator: Anton Troianovski
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio said nuclear talks with Iran may take place.

item.title: These Are 5 of the Main Issues to Be Resolved in an Iran-U.S. Peace Deal
item.description: President Trump says the U.S. and Iran could be close to a peace deal. The big issues at stake include the fate of Iran’s nuclear program and reopening the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg and Ephrat Livni
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps at a government-organized demonstration in support of Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei, the new supreme leader, in April.

item.title: Rubio Says U.S.-India Ties Are Strong, Despite Fury Over Trump’s Actions
item.description: In New Delhi, Secretary of State Marco Rubio tried to explain why President Trump has pushed aggressive trade and immigration policies affecting India and Indians in the United States.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.media:description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio, at a news conference on Sunday in New Delhi, said the relationship between India and the United States “has not lost any momentum.”

item.title: Iran Projects Victory in Potential Deal With Washington
item.description: Iran’s leaders argue that the possible preliminary agreement has not extracted any major concessions.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Iranians waving flags last month at a government-organized demonstration in Tehran, the capital.

item.title: Fate of War in Lebanon Rests Mostly With Outside Powers
item.description: A U.S.-Iran peace deal could calm the conflict in Lebanon between Israel and Hezbollah. But that will depend mainly on what the United States, Iran and Israel decide.
item.dc:creator: Christina Goldbaum
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A building hit by an Israeli strike, as seen through a broken window at the Hiram Hospital in Tyre, Lebanon, on Saturday.

item.title: At Least 14 Killed in Bombing Attack That Derailed a Train in Pakistan
item.description: The explosion was so powerful that several nearby buildings were damaged and a resident 15 miles away was jolted awake by the blast. A separatist group claimed responsibility.
item.dc:creator: Zia ur-Rehman
item.media:description: A Pakistani soldier aiming his weapon after an explosion struck a passenger train in Quetta, Pakistan, on Sunday.

item.title: Israeli Leader Says He and Trump Agree on Blocking an Iranian Nuclear Weapon
item.description: Analysts said Israel was worried that a potential agreement would not significantly degrade Iran’s nuclear and missile capabilities.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Marc Israel Sellem
item.media:description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel at a ceremony on the eve of Israel’s day of remembrance for fallen soldiers in Jerusalem in April.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager, Aaron Boxerman, Farnaz Fassihi and Julian Barnes

item.title: What to Know About the Potential U.S.-Iran Peace Deal
item.description: Iran and the United States appeared to be negotiating on a deal to end the war, but talks could take days. And both sides have offered conflicting accounts of the emerging agreement.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang, Amelia Nierenberg and Ephrat Livni
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: People walking near a billboard depicting the closure of the Strait of Hormuz in Tehran, Iran, this month.

item.title: UK Institute Is Hunting for Dangers Lurking in AI
item.description: The government’s A.I. Security Institute, staffed by alumni from OpenAI and Google, is becoming a model for countries grappling with A.I.’s emerging risks.
item.dc:creator: Adam Satariano and Paul Mozur
item.media:credit: Jeremie Souteyrat for The New York Times
item.media:description: Along Parliament Square in London is the home of the A.I. Security Institute, which employs weapons inspectors, epidemiologists and code breakers to probe the technology’s risks.

item.title: Do U.S. Automakers Have a Future in Canada?
item.description: Detroit-based automakers pioneered open trade between Canada and the United States, benefiting both countries. President Trump’s trade war now leaves their future in the country in question.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen

item.title: As Rents Soar, Londoners Turn to Vacant Pubs, Offices and Police Stations
item.description: Property guardianship, in which residents live in otherwise vacant buildings, used to be considered edgy. But as the rental market tightens, more people are embracing the arrangement.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe and Alice Zoo For The New York Times
item.media:credit: Alice Zoo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Once a boutique hotel, The Kings Arms Hotel in London is now a guardianship property.

item.title: Iranian Officials Say Proposed Peace Deal Would Open Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Iran has not formally responded to President Trump’s announcement that a peace deal was close. But officials suggested the oil choke point in the Strait of Hormuz could reopen.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A crowd waving Iranian flags during a mass wedding ceremony in Tehran last week.

item.title: Kyiv, Ukraine, Hit in Russian Missile Attack
item.description: Buildings rattled in the Ukrainian capital for hours early Sunday. Russia launched an Oreshnik intermediate-range ballistic missile for only the third time in the war.
item.dc:creator: Andrew E. Kramer
item.media:description: The widespread Russian missile attack on Sunday left entire city blocks in Kyiv ablaze.

item.title: Can Concrete Molds Revive Coral Reefs Ruined by Bombs and Climate Change?
item.description: In a devastated section of the Coral Triangle in the Pacific Ocean, a conservation group is trying to build an artificial reef.
item.dc:creator: Zunaira Saieed and Sirachai Arunrugstichai
item.media:description: Robin Philippo, the managing director of the Tropical Research and Conservation Center, installing an artificial reef structure near Pom Pom Island in Malaysia.

item.title: Iran Agreed to Give Up Enriched Uranium in Deal Announced by Trump, U.S. Officials Say
item.description: Precise details were unclear but the officials said the U.S. demanded a commitment from Iran on uranium as part of any initial agreement.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard in Tehran depicts the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Many Questions, Few Details in Latest Iran Peace Proposal
item.description: It is too early to tell what exactly Trump and Iran have agreed to, or if they have agreed to much at all.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump in New York on Friday.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.description: PresidTrump provided few details about the preliminary agreement, which he said was “largely negotiated.”
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman, Tyler Pager, Farnaz Fassihi and Julian E. Barnes
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: France Bans Israeli Minister Over Treatment of Detained Flotilla Activists
item.description: Itamar Ben-Gvir, the Israeli national security minister, has long drawn criticism for his hard-line statements and harsh policies against Palestinian prisoners.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman
item.media:description: Israel’s national security minister, Itamar Ben-Gvir, in the Israeli Parliament on Wednesday.

item.title: A Lull in Fighting Gave Time to Bury Their Dead
item.description: In the relative calm of the early days of a cease-fire, there were heart-rending scenes as people exhumed their loved ones from temporary graves and reburied them.
item.dc:creator: David Guttenfelder and Christina Goldbaum
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The human toll of the war has spared few parts of southern Lebanon.

item.title: Israeli Strikes Pummel Lebanon, Killing Medics Amid Fragile Truce
item.description: Saturday’s strikes damaged a main hospital in the Lebanese city of Tyre, as funerals for paramedics killed a day earlier were held.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Chaayto and Abdi Latif Dahir
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Mourners at the funeral of a paramedic and two civil defense workers, in Tyre, Lebanon, on Saturday. They were killed in an Israeli airstrike the day before, according to Lebanon’s health ministry.

item.title: Alexandros Giotopoulos of November 17, Greek Militant Group, Is Freed
item.description: The release of Alexandros Giotopoulos was denounced by relatives of people killed by November 17, a Greek far-left group that was active between 1975 and 2002.
item.dc:creator: Niki Kitsantonis
item.media:description: Alexandros Giotopoulos in a courtroom in an Athens prison in 2005. His lawyer said he was released on Thursday because of his age, among other reasons.

item.title: Iran and U.S. Officials Signal Progress as Cease-Fire Hangs in Balance
item.description: As people across the Middle East braced for the possibility of renewed fighting, officials from both sides said there were signs that they were moving closer to an agreement.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman, Leily Nikounazar and Adam Rasgon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A handout picture from the Islamic Consultative Assembly News Agency, showing Syed Asim Munir, Pakistan’s army chief of staff, left, meeting with Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, Iran’s chief negotiator and parliamentary speaker, on Saturday in Tehran.

item.title: White House Tells Congo’s Soccer Team to Isolate, Citing Ebola Outbreak
item.description: The team, now in Belgium, must isolate there for 21 days or risk being denied permission to come to Houston for the World Cup, an official said.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:description: The Democratic Republic of Congo national soccer team before a World Cup qualifying match against Cameroon in November, in Rabat, Morocco.

item.title: Track Changes
item.description: It’s a holiday weekend, three days to ease from one seasonal mind-set into the next, even if it feels too soon.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: At the Arctic Games, Canada and Greenland vs. Trump Feels Like Its Own Sport
item.description: Geopolitics found its way to the podium in Whitehorse as athletes from the circumpolar North competed in sports as mainstream as hockey and as unusual as the two-foot high kick.
item.dc:creator: Pat Kane
item.media:description: Petra Amossen, from Uummannaq, Greenland, left, and Nicole Johnson.

item.title: U.S. Ebola Travel Ban Faces Criticism From Congo Health Officials
item.description: Kinshasa residents continue to pack markets, bars and public transportation, despite growing international concern about the spread of the virus.
item.dc:creator: Justin Makangara and John Eligon
item.media:credit: Justin Makangara for The New York Times
item.media:description: A crowded market on Saturday in Kinshasa, the Congolese capital.

item.title: Trump’s Pursuit of a Partnership With China Raises Concerns in India
item.description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio has a “gargantuan task” during his visit to Delhi: defuse tensions over President Trump’s anti-India aggression and overtures to China.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.media:description: President Trump’s desire to become a partner of Xi Jinping has left Secretary of State Marco Rubio to explain to India’s leaders Mr. Trump’s vision for Asia.

item.title: ‘A Total Nightmare’: Voices From a Moscow Hit by Ukrainian Drones
item.description: Residents who experienced attacks in recent days were shaken deeply by the conflict coming home to the capital region, the seat of the Kremlin’s power.
item.dc:creator: Valerie Hopkins and Nanna Heitmann
item.media:description: A damaged residential building in Zelenograd, Russia, after a drone strike on Sunday.

item.title: How to Stay Safe Around Jellyfish This Beach Season
item.description: Jellyfish myths and misconceptions abound. Here’s how to stay safe this beach season around these gelatinous wonders.
item.dc:creator: Johnny Diaz
item.media:description: A jellyfish at the Steinhart Aquarium at the California Academy of Sciences in San Francisco. Jellyfish play important roles in the marine ecosystem and are a key source of food for some fish and sea turtles.

item.title: Xi Calls for All-Out Rescue After Coal Mine Explosion Kills at Least 82 in China
item.description: The Chinese leader, Xi Jinping, called for an investigation and emphasized the need to “hold those responsible to account.”
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson
item.media:description: Rescuers at the site of a deadly explosion at the Liushenyu coal mine in China’s Shanxi Province on Saturday.

item.title: ‘Amazon.com of South Korea’ Is New Source of Tension With U.S.
item.description: A corporate compliance dispute between South Korea’s government and the online retailer Coupang is testing the resilience of U.S.-South Korean ties.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun and Aaron Krolik
item.media:description: President Trump and the president of South Korea, Lee Jae Myung, last year in Gyeongju, South Korea.
